So what is top-down analysis? Top-down
analysis means starting chart analysis
with higher time frames like weekly and
daily and then moving down to smaller
details like 4hour, 1 hour and 15 minute
to get a more accurate analysis. Now why
is top- down analysis so important?
Top-down analysis is important because
it helps you understand the market
conditions better before you take any
trade. When you start from higher time
frames, you can clearly see the overall
trend, strong levels of market
structure. Then when you move down to
lower time frames, you can find cleaner
entries and avoid trades that go against
the bigger picture. In short, it keeps
you on the right side of the market,
reduces bad trades, and gives you more
confidence in your decisions.
For example, here we have a clear
downtrend on the 15-minute chart.
Placing a short trade on a pullback
might look like a great idea to follow
the trend. However, if we check the
4hour time frame, we can see that the
price has reached a key market structure
level. This means there is a higher
chance that the price will react to this
level, making you question whether the
short trade is still a good idea.
On the contrary, here we have an uptrend
on the 1 hour time frame. If we switch
to the daily time frame, we
[clears throat] can see that there is
still plenty of space before the price
reaches the next key level. So when we
go back to the 1 hour chart, we can open
a long trading setup confidently and
target the daily structure.
In the third trading example, we have a

This trading technique starts from the

Step one, start with the weekly time
frame. On this higher time frame, the
only thing we need to focus on is
identifying and marking key market
structure levels. That's all we use the
weekly chart for. We don't apply any
other concepts here because they aren't
necessary. Our main trading time frame
is the 1 hour chart and lower. The
reason we mark these levels is that when
price taps into them, we expect a strong
reaction. So, we need to see them
clearly when working on the lower time
frames. But what are market structure
levels? Market structure levels are
important price zones on a chart where
the market has previously shown a strong
reaction. Every reaction can be
identified as a market structure level.
But here are five key criteria that make
a level strong.
Number one, acted as both support and
resistance. When a market structure
level has previously acted as both
support and resistance, it becomes more
important and increases the chance that
the price will reject it again. Number
two, the move away was drastic. If the
price moved away from an area very
strongly before, it means the level is
important and the price may react
strongly there again. Number three,
multiple rejections. If an area has
multiple rejections, it means traders
reacted at that same level many times,
which makes the level more important.
More rejections mean the level is
stronger, but they do not guarantee it
will hold again. Only after a break and
retest can we confidently expect a
rejection. Number four, turning points.
Turning points are areas that have
caused the market to reverse its trend
in the past. Because the market views
these zones as overvalued or oversold,
there is a higher chance that the price
will react to them again when it
returns.
Number five, recently respected.
Recently respected or newly formed
levels are more likely to create a
strong reaction because fresh levels are
generally stronger than older ones.
Now that you understand what makes a
market structure level strong and
important, let's go back to the weekly
time frame and mark those levels on the

Now the 4hour chart is where we start

Market direction, imbalance, supply and
demand zones and liquidity levels.
Before we continue, let's briefly define
these concepts so we know exactly what
to look for on the chart and the purpose
behind each one. Number one, market
direction. When price forms a 1, two,
and three moves followed by a breakout,
we have a trend. If price breaks above
the most recent swing high again, we
have a trend continuation. As long as
the price stays above the latest
protected swing low, the market remains
in an uptrend and we stay bullish on
this pair. If we get another breakout to
the upside, we identify the lowest point
that caused that break as the new swing
low. Once again, we remain bullish as
long as the price stays above this swing
low. However, if the price manages to
break and close below the protected low,
it forms a change of character. A change
of character indicates that buyers have
failed to protect the structure and
sellers are now taking control. It
signals that a potential market reversal
is coming.
Number two, supply and demand zones.
Supply and demand zones are areas on the
chart where traders have entered the
market before causing big price moves.
Normally, we mark the beginning of these
big moves as the supply or demand area
and we expect the price to reject these
zones when it comes back to them.
However, several key factors make a
supply or demand zone stronger.
Inefficiency, break of structure, and
the push distance.
Inefficiency happens when price leaves
an area very quickly because there is an
imbalance between buying and selling
pressure. We can see this through fair
value gaps left between candles. These
gaps show that there wasn't enough
supply or demand at that level which
makes the demand zone stronger. Break of
structure as the name says happens when
price breaks a previous structure level
to the upside or downside. This shows
that the market is continuing in the
same direction and that buyers or
sellers are still in control, which
confirms the zone as a strong
continuation point.
The push distance refers to how far
price moves away from the zone. A larger
push distance usually means a stronger
rejection, making the zone more
reliable. Here is an example of a
highquality demand zone. The latest
impulsive move meets all the rules for a
strong zone. It created a fair value
gap, broke the previous market
structure, and pushed the price higher
by a big distance compared to earlier
moves. So, we can mark the first candle
that started this move as the demand
zone or mark the last red candle before
buyers stepped in. If price pulls back
to this zone, it creates a long trading
opportunity.
Number three, liquidity. Liquidity is
where stop- losses and pending orders
are placed. It exists below every swing
high and swing low.
If the price forms liquidity levels
above a demand zone, the chance of that
demand zone working becomes higher
because liquidity is needed for big
moves in the market. Same concept
applies to the supply zone. A valid
liquidity grab happens when price moves
below the area and triggers the stop
losses and then quickly comes back above
the range. When this happens, we expect
a push to the upside.
Now that we have explained the basic
smart money concepts and how to apply
them on the chart, let's return to the
4hour example on the Euro Dollar pair.
On the 4hour chart, we can see a clear

But if the price rejects the 4hour
demand zone and breaks above the daily
structure, then both the 1 hour and
4hour charts become bullish and we can

So let's play the price forward and see
